concepts.name: Sustaining Innovation
concepts.definition: An innovation that improves an established product along dimensions mainstream customers already value. It can be incremental or radical; the defining feature is customer-valued performance improvement in the existing value network.

concepts.name: Disruptive Innovation
concepts.definition: An innovation that initially underperforms on mainstream dimensions but offers a different package of attributes to a foothold market, then improves into mainstream viability.

concepts.name: Strategic Misclassification
concepts.definition: The error of treating a sustaining battle as disruption or a disruptive threat as a sustaining improvement.

arguments.claim: Incumbents usually win sustaining innovation battles.
arguments.evidence: Their resources, customer relationships, and processes are tuned to improve products for mainstream customers.

arguments.claim: Incumbents usually lose disruptive battles if they manage them inside the mainstream organization.
arguments.evidence: The disruptive opportunity looks smaller, lower-margin, and inferior by the metrics that guide the existing business.

cases.name: Mainstream product improvement vs. disruptive entry
cases.context: Disk-drive capacity improvements and cable-excavator enhancements were sustaining, while smaller form factors and hydraulics were disruptive.
cases.illustrates: The same industry can contain both kinds of innovation at once.
cases.lesson: Diagnose the type before choosing the response.

logicChains.description: Classify innovation → if sustaining, use mainstream resources and customers → if disruptive, isolate it in a context whose customers need it → measure progress by the foothold market rather than mainstream margins.

terminology.definition: Improving what current good customers already value.
Different value proposition starting outside mainstream acceptance.
A rival attacking the same customers with a better or cheaper version of what they already buy; not necessarily disruption.

nuance: Sustaining innovation can be radical; radical technology is not automatically disruptive.
Overusing the word disruption makes the theory useless for action.
